summary: Jeff Atwood traces the history of artillery games from their origins on 1960s mainframes through DOS classics like Scorched Earth to modern incarnations like the Worms series. He highlights how these games, built on simple physics mechanics of wind, angle, and power, became programming samples and learning tools for generations of developers. The post features an interview with Scorched Earth creator Wendell Hicken, who describes reverse-engineering arcade games as a child and building his own versions at home. Atwood uses this history to illustrate the deep connection between games and programming, showing how simple gameplay mechanics can inspire decades of innovation and remain compelling entertainment.
key_points: Artillery games originated on 1960s mainframes, with programmers sharing them freely via punch cards and printer output
These games were commonly used as programming samples due to their simplicity and real physics calculations, including the famous gorilla.bas bundled with MS-DOS
Scorched Earth's creator learned programming by visiting arcades with a notepad to reverse-engineer games, then implementing them at home
Simple game mechanics can sustain decades of evolution and remain commercially viable, as demonstrated by the Worms franchise
The advice for aspiring game developers is to start simple and keep learning, building incrementally on working code
money_quotes: Because they were simple, fun, and used some real physics calculations, artillery games were often used as programming samples.
There's nothing quite as satisfying as nailing your opponent with the laughably ineffective 'Funky Bomb.'
While other kids were dumping quarters into arcades, I would visit the arcades with a notepad, and try to figure out what the games were doing – then I'd go home and implement them myself.
If you have a really simple program that at least does something, then this can keep you motivated to keep making it better.
It's amazing how far a simple gameplay mechanic like wind, angle, and power can take you.
key_insight: Simple, well-designed game mechanics can inspire generations of programmers and remain compelling across decades of technological change.
